narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which stands out above the typical 3.21 average—and your family's high income bracket, along with planning to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 59%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that carried the most weight in the decision. The biggest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your LSAT score of 22.50, well below the average of 36.70, which signaled to the model a notable gap in the standardized skills often linked to bar success. Close behind was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far lower than the typical 5.53 average, suggesting your later academic performance in school raised concerns about sustaining momentum through law studies and the bar. On a more positive note, your high family income stood out as the strongest factor pulling in the opposite direction, toward a passing prediction, likely because it reflects resources that can support your preparation. Compared to typical students, where full-time enrollment is the norm at 92.7%, your choice aligns well, but it wasn't enough to offset those top concerns. Your solid undergrad GPA helped in the background but didn't rank among the heaviest hitters here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with clear drive—it's the model weighing patterns from past students to forecast bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, while strengthening your recent academic showings through additional coursework or targeted prep. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas, and law schools often value persistence. You have the foundation; refining these key pieces could open doors next cycle.
